eli3: Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM) is the standard mathematical model for stock prices. It says stock prices grow at a constant expected rate (drift) and experience random, normally distributed shocks (diffusion). The 'geometric' part ensures prices can never go negative вҖ” which makes physical sense for stocks.

The GBM equation: dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW, where S is the stock price, Ој is the expected return (drift), Пғ is the volatility, and dW is a Brownian motion increment (a tiny random shock). The key feature: percentage changes in price are normally distributed, which means log prices follow a random walk with drift вҖ” log-normal distribution for prices.

GBM is the foundation of the Black-Scholes option pricing model. It's a simplification of reality (real prices have fat tails, volatility clustering, and jumps), but it's analytically tractable and provides closed-form solutions that are foundational to derivatives pricing and risk management.

whenWhy.title: Deriving the log-normal distribution of stock prices
whenWhy.body: From dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW, apply Ito's lemma to f(S) = ln(S): d(ln S) = (Ој - ПғВІ/2) dt + Пғ dW. This shows log prices follow a Brownian motion with drift (Ој - ПғВІ/2) and diffusion Пғ. Integrating: ln(S_T) = ln(S_0) + (Ој - ПғВІ/2)T + ПғвҲҡT Г— Z, where Z ~ N(0,1). Therefore: S_T = S_0 Г— exp[(Ој - ПғВІ/2)T + ПғвҲҡT Г— Z]. S_T is log-normally distributed: ln(S_T/S_0) ~ N((Ој - ПғВІ/2)T, ПғВІT). Key: E[S_T] = S_0 Г— e^{ОјT} (grows at rate Ој); median of S_T = S_0 Г— e^{(Ој-ПғВІ/2)T} (grows slower due to the Jensen inequality correction -ПғВІ/2). The -ПғВІ/2 term is the Ito correction вҖ” it appears because variance in geometric growth reduces the median outcome.

whenWhy.title: Monte Carlo simulation of stock price paths
whenWhy.body: GBM Monte Carlo simulation: generate N paths of S_t using the discretised GBM: S_{t+О”t} = S_t Г— exp[(Ој - ПғВІ/2)О”t + ПғвҲҡО”t Г— Z_t], where Z_t ~ N(0,1) iid. Steps: (1) Choose О”t (e.g., daily = 1/252 years); (2) Generate NГ—T matrix of N(0,1) random numbers; (3) Recursively compute S_t along each path; (4) Use the distribution of S_T across paths to price options or compute VaR. Applications: (1) European option pricing (matches Black-Scholes analytically); (2) Exotic option pricing with path-dependent payoffs; (3) Portfolio risk simulation; (4) Corporate scenario analysis. Key: use variance reduction techniques (antithetic variates, quasi-random sequences) to improve convergence efficiency.

whenWhy.title: Parameter estimation from historical data
whenWhy.body: Estimate GBM parameters from historical returns: ОјМӮ = mean(daily return) Г— 252 (annualise); ПғМӮ = std(daily return) Г— вҲҡ252 (annualise). From log returns: r_t = ln(S_t/S_{t-1}) ~ N((Ој - ПғВІ/2)/252, ПғВІ/252). So mean(r_t) = (Ој - ПғВІ/2)/252 and var(r_t) = ПғВІ/252. Estimation from daily returns (T = 252 observations): Пғ is estimated precisely (Пғ grows with вҲҡT; more observations help); Ој is estimated very imprecisely (standard error вүҲ Пғ/вҲҡT вүҲ 20%/вҲҡ252 вүҲ 1.3% daily, implying Вұ26% annual confidence interval). This 'estimation problem' is fundamental: we can estimate volatility reliably from daily data but cannot estimate expected return reliably even from decades of data. Implication: portfolio optimisation is more reliable using risk (Пғ) than expected return (Ој).

deepDiveIntro: GBM SDE: dS_t = Ој S_t dt + Пғ S_t dW_t. Ito's lemma: for f(S,t), df = (вҲӮf/вҲӮt + ОјS вҲӮf/вҲӮS + ВҪПғВІSВІ вҲӮВІf/вҲӮSВІ)dt + ПғS вҲӮf/вҲӮS dW. The extra ВҪПғВІSВІ вҲӮВІf/вҲӮSВІ term (the Ito correction) arises because dWВІ = dt (not 0, as in ordinary calculus) вҖ” this is the fundamental difference between stochastic and ordinary calculus. Closed-form solution: S_T = S_0 exp[(Ој - ПғВІ/2)T + ПғW_T]. Multivariate GBM (correlated assets): dS_i = Ој_i S_i dt + Пғ_i S_i dW_i, where dW_i dW_j = ПҒ_ij dt. Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix generates correlated Brownian motions from independent ones.

deepDive.heading: GBM Limitations: Fat Tails, Volatility Clustering, and Jumps
deepDive.body: GBM assumes: (1) constant volatility; (2) normally distributed returns; (3) no jumps; (4) continuous trading. All four are violated by real asset prices. Empirical violations: (1) Return distributions have fat tails (kurtosis > 3): extreme moves occur far more frequently than GBM predicts. S&P 500: historical kurtosis вүҲ 20-30 (daily). A 5-sigma daily move in GBM probability вүҲ 1 in 3.5 million days; in reality it occurs several times per decade; (2) Volatility clustering (ARCH/GARCH): high-volatility days cluster together; Пғ is not constant; (3) Jumps: discrete price discontinuities from earnings announcements, geopolitical events; (4) Leverage effect: volatility increases when prices fall (negative correlation between returns and future volatility). Extensions: Heston (1993) stochastic volatility; Merton (1976) jump-diffusion; GARCH (Bollerslev 1986). Each addresses specific GBM failures at the cost of analytical tractability. [1]

deepDive.heading: Multi-Asset GBM and Correlation Dynamics
deepDive.body: Portfolio of assets following correlated GBMs: dS_i = Ој_i S_i dt + Пғ_i S_i dW_i with dW_i dW_j = ПҒ_ij dt. Portfolio value V = ОЈ w_i S_i. The portfolio's return distribution is not log-normal (weighted sum of log-normals). For risk calculations: use Monte Carlo. The correlation matrix ПҒ is critical: in normal times, historical correlations apply; in crises, correlations spike toward 1 (diversification fails when most needed). This is the key limitation for VaR and portfolio optimisation: using historical correlations understates crisis risk. Copulas (specifically t-copula with tail dependence) better capture crisis correlation dynamics. The 2008 crisis: correlations across global equity markets spiked above 0.9; portfolios assumed to have Пғ = 15% based on historical data actually experienced Пғ = 40%. [2]

deepDive.heading: Risk-Neutral GBM and Option Pricing
deepDive.body: For option pricing, use the risk-neutral measure where all assets grow at the risk-free rate r (not Ој): dS = r S dt + Пғ S dW^Q (under Q, the risk-neutral measure). The risk-neutral drift ensures no arbitrage вҖ” in a risk-neutral world, all investors are indifferent to risk. Black-Scholes pricing: the option price is the expected discounted payoff under Q. For a European call: C = SвӮҖN(dвӮҒ) - Ke^{-rT}N(dвӮӮ), where dвӮҒ = [ln(SвӮҖ/K) + (r + ПғВІ/2)T] / (ПғвҲҡT), dвӮӮ = dвӮҒ - ПғвҲҡT. The change from Ој to r in simulation eliminates the need to estimate the unobservable Ој for option pricing вҖ” option prices depend on Пғ (market-observable via implied vol) and r (risk-free rate), not on expected returns. This 'change of measure' (Girsanov's theorem) is the key insight of modern derivatives pricing. [1]

takeaways: GBM: dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW; log returns are normally distributed; prices are log-normally distributed. [1]
Ito correction: d(ln S) = (Ој - ПғВІ/2)dt + ПғdW; the -ПғВІ/2 term reduces geometric mean relative to arithmetic mean. [1]
Estimate Пғ precisely from historical data; Ој is essentially unknowable from any finite sample. [1]
GBM fails on fat tails, volatility clustering, and jumps вҖ” real markets have kurtosis ~20-30. [1]
Risk-neutral GBM: replace Ој with r for options pricing (Girsanov's theorem); eliminates need to estimate Ој. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does GBM fail to capture the volatility smile in options markets?
edgeCases.a: GBM with constant Пғ implies a single implied volatility for all options on the same underlying вҖ” a flat volatility surface. In reality, market-implied volatilities vary by strike and maturity, forming a 'volatility smile' (or skew). The skew (put implied vol > call implied vol at same distance from ATM) reflects: (1) fat left tail вҖ” markets price in higher probability of large declines than GBM predicts; (2) leverage effect вҖ” stocks fall faster on bad news; (3) crash risk premium вҖ” put options are expensive because they provide portfolio insurance. Extensions: local volatility models (Dupire 1994) make Пғ a deterministic function of S and t to match the smile exactly; stochastic volatility models (Heston) make Пғ itself random, generating volatility smiles naturally. Both are used in practice depending on the application.

For dividend-paying stocks, modify GBM: dS = (Ој - q)S dt + ПғS dW, where q is the continuous dividend yield. The stock price falls by q continuously (dividends are paid out, reducing intrinsic value). For discrete dividends: subtract the present value of expected dividends from SвӮҖ before applying GBM. In Black-Scholes for dividend-paying stocks (Merton 1973): replace SвӮҖ with SвӮҖe^{-qT} in the formula. For equity index options: q is the index dividend yield (typically 1.5-2.5% for S&P 500). This adjustment matters especially for long-dated options where dividend yield has significant impact on pricing. Currencies: treat the foreign risk-free rate r_f as the 'dividend yield' вҖ” the Garman-Kohlhagen model extends Black-Scholes to FX options this way.

sources.label: Black, F. & Scholes, M. (1973) вҖ” The Pricing of Options and Corporate Liabilities, JPE
Hull, J.C. вҖ” Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (10th ed.), Pearson
